channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Appeals Court Stops V.O.A. Journalists From Quickly Returning
item.description: A panel of three judges halted the lower court order to reinstate all full-time Voice of America reporters and support staff who had been put on paid leave.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Agency for Global Media
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voice of America office building in Washington.

item.title: Bomb Shelters and a Drone-Proof Roof: Trump Says Ballroom Is a Matter of Security
item.description: President Trump spoke about his ballroom’s security features as he argued against a judge’s orders to stop construction.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge on Tuesday said that work on the White House ballroom had to stop until the project received a go-ahead from Congress.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Will Be Out of Iran Within Two to Three Weeks
item.description: The White House said the president would address the nation about Iran on Wednesday evening.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump told reporters on Tuesday that he had attained his goal of dealing with Iran’s nuclear program and that gasoline prices in the United States would be coming down.

item.title: Trump Says He Halted Nuclear Threat From Iran, Despite Evidence to the Contrary
item.description: For the second time in recent days, President Trump declared that one of the key objectives of the war had been accomplished.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has narrowed his Iran war objectives in recent days.

item.title: Trump Administration Acted Illegally With Homeless Grants Program, Judge Rules
item.description: A federal judge in Rhode Island said that the administration had wrongly demanded that groups seeking grants comply with its agenda on other issues, including immigration.
item.dc:creator: Jason DeParle
item.media:credit: Jim Wilson/The New York Times
item.media:description: San Francisco officials clear homeless encampments from city streets after a Supreme Court ruling.

item.title: South Dakotans React to Daily Mail Article on Bryon Noem, Kristi Noem’s Husband
item.description: In the tiny town of Castlewood, S.D., where everyone knows the Noems, the prevailing sense was that people can’t help but feel bad for Bryon Noem after a tabloid photo leak.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Ben Brewer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Castlewood is not far from the Noem family farm in South Dakota.

item.title: Trump Signs Order Seeking Federal Control of Mail Voting as He Promotes False Claims
item.description: Election experts and Democratic officials called the order legally invalid, and Arizona and Oregon pledged to immediately challenge it in court.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times

item.title: Tugboat Captain Is Charged in Miami Beach Crash That Killed 3 Children
item.description: Six people, including a camp counselor, were thrown into the water on July 28 when their sailboat was struck by a barge that was being pushed by a tugboat, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz and Mark Walker
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images
item.media:description: An aerial view of Biscayne Bay surrounding Hibiscus Island in Miami Beach, where a barge struck a sailboat in July, killing three children.

item.title: Judge Dismisses Lawsuit That Challenged Ban on Endorsements by Churches
item.description: Conservatives had expected a victory in the case after the I.R.S. agreed to a settlement that allowed churches to voice support for candidates.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge in Texas on Tuesday dismissed a lawsuit seeking to overturn the 70-year-old ban on political activity by churches.

item.title: Justice Dept. Struggles to Respond to Trump’s Suit Against IRS
item.description: Officials at the department and the White House are in the middle of a messy and complicated debate over how to respond to President Trump’s lawsuit demanding $10 billion from the I.R.S.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump filed a lawsuit demanding at least $10 billion from the I.R.S.

item.title: Backdoor Funding of Homeland Security Agency Could Weaken Congress Anew
item.description: An emerging Republican plan to skirt a Democratic filibuster and fund an entire department without congressional appropriations would be the latest example of surrendering power to the White House.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Border Patrol agents in Minneapolis in early January. Using the complex budget process known as reconciliation to fund the entire Homeland Security Department, as Republicans are suggesting, would be a significant departure from traditional congressional practice.

item.title: Trump’s Presidential Library Video Draws Mixed Reactions From Miami Residents
item.description: The president’s foundation released a video showing a gleaming tower by Biscayne Bay emblazoned with his name. It would dominate the skyline.
item.dc:creator: Audra D. S. Burch, David C. Adams and Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Kirby Lee/Imagn Images, via Reuters Connect
item.media:description: President Trump plans to use the prime property with bay views donated to him by the state of Florida to maximum effect.

item.title: Who Is Getting Paid During the Department of Homeland Security Shutdown?
item.description: At least 120,000 law enforcement officers who work for the agency have continued to collect paychecks throughout the funding lapse. But tens of thousands of workers have gone without pay.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Gene J. Puskar/Associated Press
item.media:description: A T.S.A. agent checks passengers at the security checkpoint at Pittsburgh International Airport on Monday.

item.title: Gavin Newsom Suggests His Conservative Critics Are Gay in Online Insults Criticized as Homophobic
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom’s aides said that their online insults were meant to ridicule figures on the right. But some critics say they are homophobic.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Felix Hörhager/picture-alliance, via AP Images
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California, a potential 2028 presidential candidate, is attracting criticism for social media posts taunting his opponents by suggesting they are gay.

item.title: Judge Orders Construction Stopped on Trump’s White House Ballroom
item.description: A federal judge required the president to seek lawmakers’ input and pursue traditional approvals before proceeding with the $400 million replacement for the East Wing.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge ordered on Tuesday that construction be halted of the White House ballroom.

item.title: Judge Rules Trump Unlawfully Revoked Status of Migrants Who Used Entry App
item.description: A federal judge in Massachusetts ordered the Trump administration to reinstate the legal status of those who were invited to use the Biden-era app.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: The app allowed migrants to schedule appointments for inspection at a port of entry.

item.title: Judge Approves Trump Effort to Obtain List of Jews From University of Pennsylvania
item.description: The government’s effort to collect the names and phone numbers of Jewish people on campus as it investigates antisemitism has upset some people who worry about how the information will be used.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Some Jewish students and faculty at the University of Pennsylvania have expressed concern about the Trump administration’s tactics.

item.title: Trump Seeks to Redefine ‘Regime Change’ in Iran War
item.description: President Trump and his aides have made contradictory statements on whether the United States and Israel have transformed the Iranian government through violence.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said during his news conference on Tuesday that “this new regime, because regime change has occurred, should be wiser than the last.”

item.title: TMZ Goes After Members of Congress Living It Up Amid DHS Shutdown
item.description: After lawmakers left Washington for a two-week spring break with the Department of Homeland Security shut down, the Hollywood tabloid began publishing photographs of them living it up around the country.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times

item.title: Fired F.B.I. Agents File Class-Action Suit Claiming Political Retaliation
item.description: The suit is one of the broadest bids yet seeking accountability against the F.B.I. director, Kash Patel, and Attorney General Pam Bondi for getting rid of employees who have run afoul of President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (2020 Election Case)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The lawsuit against Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, was filed by three agents who served on a public corruption squad at the F.B.I.’s Washington field office that investigated President Trump’s efforts to cling to power after losing the 2020 election.

item.title: New Book by JD Vance Will Explore His Conversion to Catholicism
item.description: The vice president’s book, to be released in June, will detail his return to Christianity after leaving the loosely evangelical practice of his childhood.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Elizabeth Dias
item.category: Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance’s faith has become a conspicuous part of his political biography.

item.title: Pentagon Weighs Using Anti-Drone Lasers Near D.C. Military Base
item.description: Sightings of drones around Fort McNair, the Army base where Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State Marco Rubio live, have prompted consideration of deploying the new technology.
item.dc:creator: Kate Kelly, Eric Schmitt and Tyler Pager
item.category: Laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: The LOCUST, an anti-drone laser system that has been used recently along the border with Mexico, may be deployed in Washington to help protect the military base where Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State Marco Rubio reside.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Colorado Law Banning ‘Conversion Therapy’ for L.G.B.T.Q. Minors
item.description: The state and more than 20 others restrict therapists from trying to change the gender identity or sexual orientation of clients under 18.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Rachel Woolf for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kaley Chiles, a licensed professional counselor, at her office in Colorado Springs in September. Mrs. Chiles filed suit in 2022 over Colorado’s ban on conversion therapy for minors.

item.title: Trump Posts Video of Presidential Library in Miami
item.description: President Trump posted a video rendering that appeared to include elements generated by artificial intelligence of a skyscraper in Miami featuring what appeared to be Air Force One.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: Trump, Donald J, Presidential Library and Museum

item.title: Will $4 Gas Hurt Trump’s Approval Ratings? Here’s What History Shows.
item.description: Presidents since at least the Carter administration have seen their approval ratings tied to gas prices. But there are signs the correlation may be weakening.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik and Katherine Chui
item.media:credit: Katherine Chui/The New York Times

item.title: Trump Faces a Decision on Whether to Start a Ground War in Iran
item.description: The president wants a negotiation, but the Iranians say they are refusing until a cease-fire is declared. And while Marines and the 82nd Airborne Division offer new leverage, the risks escalate quickly.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and his tight circle of close aides have made opening the Strait of Hormuz a new, and nonnegotiable, issue.

item.title: Drivers Count Their Pennies as Gas Hits $4 a Gallon Because of War in Iran
item.description: Since the Iran conflict began on Feb. 28, gas prices across the United States have increased about 35 percent. They are now above $4 a gallon, and drivers are wincing.
item.dc:creator: Audra D. S. Burch, Andy Newman, Edgar Sandoval, Anna Griffin and Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Harlingen, Texas, and everywhere else, drivers are strategizing about how to stay on the road at a reasonable price.

item.title: On What Was Once Chavez Day, Some Try to Highlight a Movement, Not a Man
item.description: The reconsideration of the legacy of Cesar Chavez after he was accused of abusing women and girls has led some to question the lionization of icons, when reality is so often a letdown.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla, Jill Cowan and Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:credit: Barton Silverman/The New York Times
item.media:description: Cesar Chavez speaking in New York City in 1971.

item.title: Latino Republicans in South Texas Turn on Trump Over Birthright Citizenship
item.description: Frustrated by the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown, some Latino voters say they also disagree with his plan, now before the Supreme Court, to reject automatic citizenship for children born in the United States to immigrant parents.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Samuel Garza, 62, an actor, was born in the United States to immigrant parents. He is dismayed about the Trump administration’s proposed changes to birthright citizenship.

item.title: Surviving Cesar Chavez
item.description: Debra Rojas recently revealed that Cesar Chavez began sexually abusing her at age 12. Now, at 66, she has come forward with her story. In her first on-camera interview, she spoke with The Times about how what happened has shaped her life.
item.dc:creator: Manny Fernandez, Sarah Hurtes, Mimi Dwyer, Yasu Tsuji and Lauren Pruitt

item.title: How Immigration Policies Like Birthright Citizenship Shaped the Supreme Court Justices’ Histories
item.description: As the justices prepare to hear a landmark case about birthright citizenship, their family stories are a reminder that the law has shaped who can be an American.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle, Julie Tate and Ashley Cai

item.title: The Supreme Court’s Personal Connections to Immigration
item.description: As the court prepares to hear oral arguments in a landmark case on birthright citizenship, our Supreme Court correspondent, Abbie VanSickle, traced the justices’ ancestries. She found family histories that spanned the American experience, including one case of birthright citizenship.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle, Christina Shaman, June Kim, Whitney Shefte, Stephanie Swart, Ashley Cai and Jenni Lee

item.title: Most Americans Favor Birthright Citizenship. That Wasn’t Always True.
item.description: In the 1990s, Democrats were split in their support for birthright citizenship, and even proposed legislation to end it.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters stood outside of the Supreme Court last May in support of birthright citizenship.

item.title: For Trump, the Artemis II Moon Mission Offers a Shot to Cement His Legacy
item.description: No president since the Apollo era has pushed harder to return to the moon than President Trump. But he wants a space achievement that is about “more than getting rocks this time.”
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump visiting the Kennedy Space Center for a SpaceX launch during his first term, in May 2020.
